name: NOAA National Marine Fisheries Service (NOAA Fisheries)
summary: Federal agency (within NOAA) that stewards the nation’s ocean fisheries, protected species, and marine habitats, conducts science, sets and implements management under the Magnuson-Stevens Act, and works with Councils, states, and international partners. Source: fisheries.noaa.gov.

provenance.notes: Headquarters in Silver Spring, MD area; also see regional offices. NOAA regions page: https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/regions

name: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service
summary: Federal agency within the Department of the Interior that conserves, protects, and enhances fish, wildlife, plants, and their habitats, including the National Wildlife Refuge System, ESA, and international treaty programs. Source: fws.gov.

name: U.S. Forest Service
summary: Federal agency (USDA) that manages 193 million acres of national forests and grasslands, balancing timber, recreation, watersheds, and wildlife habitat, with a major role in public land stewardship and NRM. Source: fs.usda.gov.

provenance.notes: National HQ; forest regions nationwide.

name: Bureau of Land Management
summary: Federal land management agency (Interior) for more public surface acres than any other U.S. agency, managing rangeland, oil and gas, minerals, recreation, and wildlife habitat in multiple use under law and NEPA. Source: blm.gov.

name: National Park Service
summary: Federal bureau (Interior) that preserves park units, cultural resources, and natural systems and provides visitor services, science, and resource stewardship in national parks and related sites. Source: nps.gov.

name: The Nature Conservancy
summary: Global nonprofit that protects lands and waters through science, public-private partnerships, and easements, with a major U.S. program for habitat conservation, climate, and water security for people and nature. Source: nature.org.

name: National Audubon Society
summary: U.S. nonprofit that uses science, policy, and community engagement to protect birds, wildlife, and the habitats on which they depend, including working lands, coasts, and climate refugia. Source: audubon.org.

name: Defenders of Wildlife
summary: U.S. nonprofit that protects native animals and their habitats in North America through legal advocacy, on-the-ground conservation, and policy work focused on keystone, carnivore, and migratory species. Source: defenders.org.

name: Ducks Unlimited, Inc.
summary: Non-profit conservation organization dedicated to the conservation, restoration, and management of wetlands and waterfowl habitat in North America, with science-based delivery of habitat projects. Source: ducks.org.

provenance.notes: Memphis, TN; national and Canada programs (see international site for Canada career pages).

name: Trout Unlimited
summary: Non-profit dedicated to the conservation, protection, and restoration of North America’s coldwater fisheries and their watersheds, through science, on-the-ground projects, and volunteer chapters. Source: tu.org.

name: American Fisheries Society
summary: Professional society that advances global fisheries, aquatic resource science, and NRM through journals, standards, and meetings; supports careers and public understanding of fish resources. Source: fisheries.org.

name: National Fish and Wildlife Foundation
summary: Congressional chartered nonprofit (NFWF) that works with the public and private sector to protect and restore fish, wildlife, plants, and habitats in the U.S. through grantmaking and public-private delivery. Source: nfwf.org.

name: Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources
summary: The lead state agency in Alabama for conservation of fish, game, and aquatic and terrestrial habitats, including public lands, hunting and fishing, and law enforcement on natural resources.

provenance.notes: State natural resource or fish and wildlife lead agency. Cross-check: Association of Fish & Wildlife Agencies state directory https://www.fishwildlife.org/ ; some careers route through state civil service portals.

name: Alaska Department of Fish and Game
